header_hub.title: Hardware Interrupts Routed to Software Signals

content_blocks.content: C programs are designed to execute synchronously, line by line. But the real world is entirely asynchronous. What happens if the user physically presses Ctrl+C on their keyboard? What happens if the CPU's hardware MMU detects your program attempting to read unmapped memory (a Segmentation Fault)? The Operating System cannot wait for your program to finish its current loop to inform you of these events. The OS Kernel must violently and instantly interrupt your process's execution flow. In POSIX systems, this asynchronous interruption mechanism is achieved using Signals.

Signals are primitive, integer-based software interrupts (e.g., SIGINT = 2, SIGSEGV = 11, SIGKILL = 9). When the Kernel decides to deliver a signal to your process, it literally hijacks your CPU thread. It pauses your current C instruction, pushes a special "Signal Frame" onto your Stack containing your current CPU registers, and forcefully changes the CPU Instruction Pointer (PC) to jump to a Signal Handler function. Once the handler function finishes and returns, the Kernel restores your saved registers and resumes your original code exactly where it left off, as if nothing happened. If you do not register a custom handler, the Kernel executes a Default Action (usually terminating the process instantly).

content_blocks.caption: An execution timeline showing the main thread abruptly halted by the Kernel to execute the Signal Handler function asynchronously.
content_blocks.alt_text: Visual showing the Main Execution Context running, a Signal arriving, the context switching to a Signal Handler, and then returning to Main Execution.

questions.question: What physically happens to the CPU execution state when an unblocked Signal arrives at a running process?

options.text: The process spawns a new background thread to handle the signal.
options.explanation: Signals do not inherently spawn threads; they hijack the existing thread.

options.text: The OS Kernel instantly suspends the current instruction, pushes the CPU registers onto the stack, and forcibly jumps the Instruction Pointer to the registered Signal Handler function.
options.explanation: This asynchronous hijack forces the application to deal with the signal immediately.

options.text: The process safely finishes its current function before checking the signal queue.
options.explanation: Signals do not wait for functions to finish; they interrupt mid-execution.

options.text: The process ignores the signal unless the programmer explicitly calls `read_signal()`.
options.explanation: Signals are handled asynchronously by the Kernel, not synchronously polled.

header_hub.title: Trapping Signals using sigaction (vs deprecated signal())

content_blocks.content: Historically, programmers registered signal handlers using the signal(SIGINT, my_handler) function. This function is universally deprecated in modern systems engineering. The original signal() API was implemented inconsistently across UNIX flavors (System V vs BSD). On some systems, handling a signal automatically un-registered the handler, requiring you to re-register it inside the handler itself, which creates a fatal Race Condition if a second signal arrives before re-registration. Furthermore, signal() provided no reliable way to block other signals while the handler was running. To achieve safe, deterministic signal trapping across all POSIX compliant operating systems, we must use the robust sigaction() system call.

content_blocks.signature: #include <signal.h> struct sigaction sa; sa.sa_handler = handle_sigint; sigemptyset(&sa.sa_mask); sa.sa_flags = SA_RESTART; sigaction(SIGINT, &sa, NULL);
content_blocks.description: The modern POSIX standard for safely inspecting and modifying the action associated with a specific signal.

parameters.name: sa_handler
parameters.description: The callback function to execute when the signal arrives (e.g., void handle_sigint(int sig)).

parameters.name: sa_mask
parameters.description: A set of signals that will be physically blocked by the Kernel while this specific handler is executing, preventing overlapping handler chaos.

parameters.name: SA_RESTART
parameters.description: Instructs the Kernel to automatically restart any blocking syscalls (like read() or accept()) that were interrupted by this signal, rather than failing them with EINTR.

returns.description: Returns 0 on success, or -1 on failure (setting errno).

content_blocks.complexity: O(1) system call. Permanently modifies the process's Signal Table.
content_blocks.exceptions: You CANNOT catch or ignore SIGKILL (9) or SIGSTOP (19). The Kernel enforces these mathematically to ensure rogue processes can always be terminated by administrators.

content_blocks.header: Safely Catching SIGINT to Prevent Database Corruption (Assessment Check)

questions.question: Why is the legacy `signal()` function banned in modern C engineering architectures in favor of `sigaction()`?

options.explanation: Memory consumption is negligible for both.

options.text: Because `signal()` behaves inconsistently across OS flavors, suffers from fatal Race Conditions due to single-shot execution requiring re-registration, and offers no mechanism to block other signals during handler execution.
options.explanation: `sigaction()` provides rigorous, deterministic control over the Kernel's signal masking logic.
Neither requires root to catch standard signals sent to the user's own processes.

header_hub.title: Reentrancy Rules: What functions are Async-Signal-Safe?

content_blocks.content: When you are inside a Signal Handler, you are operating in an incredibly dangerous context. You must remember that the signal hijacked your main thread. Imagine your main thread was in the middle of executing printf(), and printf() had just acquired the internal glibc Mutex lock on the stdout stream. A SIGINT suddenly arrives. The Kernel halts the thread and jumps to your Signal Handler. Inside the handler, you decide to log a message by calling printf("Caught signal!");. The handler's printf() tries to acquire the stdout Mutex lock. But the lock is already held by the paused main thread! The handler goes to sleep waiting for the lock, but the main thread can never wake up to release it because it's suspended until the handler finishes. You have just permanently Deadlocked your entire application.

You are mathematically forbidden from calling any function inside a Signal Handler that is not Async-Signal-Safe. A function is only Async-Signal-Safe if it is Reentrant—meaning it does not use static data, does not use Mutex locks, and does not invoke malloc() or free(). The POSIX standard strictly defines a whitelist of functions you are allowed to call inside a handler. write() is safe. read() is safe. _exit() is safe. printf(), malloc(), free(), and almost the entire C Standard Library are DANGEROUS AND BANNED.
Analyze the following broken snippet. Find the vulnerability before expanding the solution below.

items.question: Reveal Post-Mortem Analysis
items.answer: The engineer committed two catastrophic errors. First, `printf()` utilizes internal locks and static buffers. If the main thread was interrupted inside a `printf` call, calling `printf` in the handler guarantees an instantaneous deadlock. Second, the standard `exit()` function invokes `fflush()` on all open `FILE*` streams and runs registered `atexit()` handlers, which also rely on Mutex locks. Fix: Inside a signal handler, if you must print data, you must use the raw POSIX `write()` syscall (e.g., `write(STDOUT_FILENO, "Alarm\n", 6);`). If you must terminate the program immediately from within the handler, you must use `_exit()` (the raw syscall that bypasses all C library cleanup).

content_blocks.title: Staff Engineer Invariant Laws
content_blocks.content: • The Volatile Sig-Atomic Rule: The absolute safest way to handle a signal is to do virtually nothing. Declare a global volatile sig_atomic_t flag = 0;. Inside your handler, simply execute flag = 1; and return immediately. Your main application loop should periodically check this flag and execute the heavy cleanup logic (like closing databases or freeing memory) securely in the synchronous main thread. The volatile keyword prevents compiler caching, and sig_atomic_t mathematically guarantees the CPU will write the integer in a single, uninterruptible clock cycle.

questions.question: Why is executing `printf()` or `malloc()` inside a Signal Handler considered a fatal architectural flaw?

options.text: They consume too much stack memory.
options.explanation: Stack memory usage is not the primary mechanism of failure here.

options.text: They are not Async-Signal-Safe. Both functions utilize internal Mutex locks. If the main thread was holding the lock when the signal interrupted it, calling the function again inside the handler will cause an unrecoverable Deadlock.
options.explanation: Signal handlers must only use Reentrant functions (like raw POSIX `write`).

options.text: They clear the signal mask automatically.
options.explanation: Signal masks are controlled by the `sigaction` struct, not standard library calls.

options.text: They execute in a separate process space.
options.explanation: The handler executes in the exact same process space, which is why the locks collide.
